retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] DetectGPT leverages the negative curvature property of large language models' log probability functions—where LLM-generated text tends to reside in regions of negative curvature (e.g., local maxima)—to detect machine-generated text without training, datasets, or watermarking. It computes the average log probability of a passage under the source model versus perturbed versions generated by a generic model (e.g., T5), using the log probability ratio to identify if the original text lies in a high-curvature region typical of LLM outputs. This curvature-based approach outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving 0.95 AUROC in detecting GPT-NeoX-generated fake news versus 0.81 for prior methods.

The increasing fluency and widespread usage of large language models (LLMs) highlight the desirability of corresponding tools aiding detection of LLM-generated text. In this paper, we identify a property of the structure of an LLM's probability function that is useful for such detection. Specifically, we demonstrate that text sampled from an LLM tends to occupy negative curvature regions of the model's log probability function. Leveraging this observation, we then define a new curvature-based criterion for judging if a passage is generated from a given LLM. This approach, which we call DetectGPT, does not require training a separate classifier, collecting a dataset of real or generated passages, or explicitly watermarking generated text. It uses only log probabilities computed by the model of interest and random perturbations of the passage from another generic pre-trained language model (e.g., T5). We find DetectGPT is more discriminative than existing zero-shot methods for model sample detection, notably improving detection of fake news articles generated by 20B parameter GPT-NeoX from 0.81 AUROC for the strongest zero-shot baseline to 0.95 AUROC for DetectGPT. See https://ericmitchell.ai/detectgpt for code, data, and other project information.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] Fast-DetectGPT introduces a novel zero-shot detection framework based on conditional probability curvature, which captures the distinct token selection patterns between humans and machines. Instead of perturbing text via expensive model calls (as in DetectGPT), it efficiently samples alternative word choices and evaluates their conditional probabilities, reducing computational cost by 340x while improving detection accuracy by ~75% in both white-box and black-box settings, with superior performance in black-box scenarios where it outperforms DetectGPT's white-box results by 28%.

Large language models (LLMs) have shown the ability to produce fluent and cogent content, presenting both productivity opportunities and societal risks. To build trustworthy AI systems, it is imperative to distinguish between machine-generated and human-authored content. The leading zero-shot detector, DetectGPT, showcases commendable performance but is marred by its intensive computational costs. In this paper, we introduce the concept of conditional probability curvature to elucidate discrepancies in word choices between LLMs and humans within a given context. Utilizing this curvature as a foundational metric, we present **Fast-DetectGPT**, an optimized zero-shot detector, which substitutes DetectGPT's perturbation step with a more efficient sampling step. Our evaluations on various datasets, source models, and test conditions indicate that Fast-DetectGPT not only surpasses DetectGPT by a relative around 75% in both the white-box and black-box settings but also accelerates the detection process by a factor of 340, as detailed in Table 1. See \\url{https://github.com/baoguangsheng/fast-detect-gpt} for code, data, and results.

parsed_candidate.text:
**LLMs for Automated Scientific Research.** The use of large language models (LLMs) to automate or assist scientific research has attracted considerable attention. Luo et al. [1] present the first systematic survey mapping LLM applications across the full research cycle, including hypothesis discovery, experiment planning, scientific writing, and peer review. The AI Scientist [2] introduces a fully automated, prompt-driven pipeline where frontier LLMs generate research ideas, write and execute code, produce experimental results, compose full papers, and perform simulated peer review—all at low computational cost. MLR-Copilot [3] proposes a three-phase LLM-agent framework combining IdeaAgent for hypothesis generation, ExperimentAgent for implementation, and iterative debugging with human feedback. Despite these advances, most existing systems rely on commercial, closed-source models as backends, which limits reproducibility and prevents the use of reinforcement learning (RL)-based policy optimization for iterative improvement. Si et al. [4] conduct the first large-scale human study comparing LLM-generated and human-expert research ideas, finding that LLM ideas are rated as more novel but slightly less feasible, while also exposing critical limitations in LLM self-evaluation and generation diversity. These findings motivate the need for trainable, open-source models that can be refined through iterative feedback.

**LLMs for Automated Peer Review.** A parallel line of work investigates whether LLMs can assist or automate the peer review process. Liu and Shah [5] provide an early exploratory study of GPT-4 for reviewing tasks, finding that LLMs show promise for error detection and checklist verification but struggle with holistic quality judgments. D'Arcy et al. [6] propose MARG, a multi-agent review generation framework that distributes paper text across specialized LLM instances, enabling full-text processing beyond context-length limits and producing more specific, helpful feedback. Yu et al. [7] introduce SEA, a standardized framework for automated peer review that normalizes heterogeneous reviews and incorporates self-correction via mismatch scoring. Tyser et al. [8] develop AI-driven review systems with bias-aware design, meta-prompting, and role-playing strategies, evaluating alignment with human preferences through pairwise comparisons. More recently, Zeng et al. [9] apply reinforcement learning to train review models with composite reward functions combining review quality and rating consistency, achieving improved factual grounding and analytical depth. Broader surveys on LLM-as-a-Judge [10, 11] provide comprehensive taxonomies of using LLMs for evaluation, highlighting challenges in consistency, bias, and domain generalization. However, these approaches typically treat peer review as a standalone task rather than integrating it into a closed-loop system where review feedback directly drives iterative improvement of research output.

**Preference Optimization for LLM Alignment.** Aligning LLMs with human preferences has been a major focus of recent research. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) traditionally requires training a separate reward model followed by policy optimization using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [12] simplifies this by reparameterizing the reward function, enabling direct policy learning from preference pairs via a classification loss. Building on DPO, SimPO [13] introduces a reference-free reward based on the average log probability of sequences, eliminating the need for a reference model and improving alignment performance. Self-Rewarding Language Models [14] propose an iterative framework where the LLM acts as its own judge, generating preference data for DPO training and improving both instruction-following and self-evaluation capabilities across iterations. Dong et al. [15] present a practical workflow for online iterative RLHF using proxy preference models, demonstrating that iterative policy updates with freshly sampled data outperform offline approaches. Our work extends these ideas to the domain of scientific research by constructing a self-contained iterative loop where a reviewer model provides preference signals to optimize a researcher model, analogous to but distinct from general-purpose self-rewarding frameworks.

**LLM-Assisted Scientific Writing.** Beyond the full research pipeline, LLMs have also been applied to specific writing tasks in science. AutoSurvey [16] automates comprehensive literature survey generation through a multi-LLM pipeline with retrieval-augmented generation, addressing context-length limitations and factual hallucination risks. The broader survey by Luo et al. [1] systematically reviews LLM applications in scientific writing, including citation generation, abstract composition, and full-paper drafting. Our work differs by training models to generate complete research papers with structured outlines, simulated experimental results, and LaTeX formatting, conditioned on a corpus of references and their abstracts.

**AI-Generated Text Detection.** As LLM-generated content becomes increasingly prevalent, detecting machine-written text—particularly in academic settings—has emerged as a critical safeguard. Bao et al. [17] propose Fast-DetectGPT, an efficient zero-shot detector based on conditional probability curvature that distinguishes human and machine-authored text without requiring training data or watermarking. This approach achieves substantial improvements in both accuracy and computational efficiency over prior perturbation-based methods such as DetectGPT [18]. We adopt Fast-DetectGPT as a component of our ethical safeguards to ensure transparency regarding the provenance of AI-generated research outputs.

tldr.text: This is the first systematic survey on Large Language Models (LLMs) in scientific research, mapping their application across four core stages: hypothesis discovery (via literature-based and inductive reasoning), experiment planning (design and execution), automated scientific writing (citation, related work, drafting), and peer review. It analyzes task-specific methodologies, evaluation benchmarks, and identifies key challenges such as domain specificity, factual accuracy, and reproducibility, while proposing future directions including agentic reasoning and integration with domain-specific knowledge graphs.

tldr.text: MLR-Copilot introduces a three-phase LLM-agent framework for autonomous machine learning research: (1) IdeaAgent generates hypothesis and experimental plans from research papers by extracting tasks, gaps, and keywords; (2) ExperimentAgent translates plans into executable code by retrieving prototype implementations, models, and data; (3) Execution phase runs experiments with iterative debugging and human feedback to refine results. The system demonstrates feasibility across five ML tasks, outperforming prior work by addressing full research cycles—including novelty, grounding in literature, and iterative validation—without relying on predefined templates or limited experimentation.

parsed_ground_truth.text:
LLMs for Research.
In recent years, several studies have explored using language models for creative tasks in research, such as multi-agent collaborative writing (Baek et al., 2024) and multi-module retrieval (Yang et al., 2023) to improve research idea generation. These works aim to boost the novelty and diversity of AI in creative tasks. Si et al. (2024) conducted a comprehensive human evaluation of the task of idea generation by language models. Wang et al. (2024b) proposed using LLMs to automatically write survey papers. Additionally, LLMs have been used to automate the research process: Huang et al. (2024) introduced a benchmark for evaluating LLMs in coding solutions for machine learning problems; Wang et al. (2023b) proposed a method leveraging LLMs for scientific literature retrieval. The AI Scientist project (Lu et al., 2024) introduced a fully automated, prompt-driven research pipeline. However, prompt-based methods often fail to generate ideas that are both diverse and practical, limiting their real-world application. To address this, we developed an iterative self-rewarding framework that enables the LLM to refine its ideas continuously, enhancing both diversity and practicality in research proposal generation.

LLMs for Science Discovery.
The tradition of Al-assisted scientific discovery (Langley, 1987; 2024) has a long history. As early as the last century, Al was applied in fields such as chemistry (Buchanan & Feigenbaum, 1981), synthetic biology (Jumper et al., 2021; Hayes et al., 2024), material discovery (Pyzer-Knapp et al., 2022; Merchant et al., 2023), and mathematics (Romera-Paredes et al., 2024). With the development of neural networks (LeCun et al., 2015), more researchers have focused on AI4Science (A14Science & Quantum, 2023; LI, 2024; Yakaboski et al., 2023). Al is mainly used for data analysis within a single domain, playing a passive role without driving scientific discovery. The key challenge is enabling Al to go beyond analysis and actively contribute to generating new research ideas, which demands advanced reasoning and creativity. Our work builds on Al's historical role in science, aiming to shift Al from a supporting tool to a leader in scientific discovery.

Automated Evaluation of Research Papers.
The use of AI tools in the scientific publishing process has garnered widespread attention (Bao et al., 2021; Liu & Shah, 2023; Liang et al., 2024; D'Arcy et al., 2024), including summarizing research paper content (Collins et al., 2017), detecting inaccuracies (Nuijten et al., 2016), and identifying fairness disparities (Zhang et al., 2022). Hosseini & Horbach (2023) conducted small-scale qualitative experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of ChatGPT in the peer review process, while Robertson (2023) invited 10 participants to assess the benefits of GPT-4 in assisting with peer review. Lu et al. (2024) and Tyser et al. (2024) used GPT-4 to evaluate full-text PDFs of scientific papers. paragraphs.text:
LLMs for Research.
In recent years, several studies have explored using language models for creative tasks in research, such as multi-agent collaborative writing (Baek et al., 2024) and multi-module retrieval (Yang et al., 2023) to improve research idea generation. These works aim to boost the novelty and diversity of AI in creative tasks. Si et al. (2024) conducted a comprehensive human evaluation of the task of idea generation by language models. Wang et al. (2024b) proposed using LLMs to automatically write survey papers. Additionally, LLMs have been used to automate the research process: Huang et al. (2024) introduced a benchmark for evaluating LLMs in coding solutions for machine learning problems; Wang et al. (2023b) proposed a method leveraging LLMs for scientific literature retrieval. The AI Scientist project (Lu et al., 2024) introduced a fully automated, prompt-driven research pipeline. However, prompt-based methods often fail to generate ideas that are both diverse and practical, limiting their real-world application. To address this, we developed an iterative self-rewarding framework that enables the LLM to refine its ideas continuously, enhancing both diversity and practicality in research proposal generation.

LLMs for Science Discovery.
The tradition of Al-assisted scientific discovery (Langley, 1987; 2024) has a long history. As early as the last century, Al was applied in fields such as chemistry (Buchanan & Feigenbaum, 1981), synthetic biology (Jumper et al., 2021; Hayes et al., 2024), material discovery (Pyzer-Knapp et al., 2022; Merchant et al., 2023), and mathematics (Romera-Paredes et al., 2024). With the development of neural networks (LeCun et al., 2015), more researchers have focused on AI4Science (A14Science & Quantum, 2023; LI, 2024; Yakaboski et al., 2023). Al is mainly used for data analysis within a single domain, playing a passive role without driving scientific discovery. The key challenge is enabling Al to go beyond analysis and actively contribute to generating new research ideas, which demands advanced reasoning and creativity. Our work builds on Al's historical role in science, aiming to shift Al from a supporting tool to a leader in scientific discovery.

Automated Evaluation of Research Papers.
The use of AI tools in the scientific publishing process has garnered widespread attention (Bao et al., 2021; Liu & Shah, 2023; Liang et al., 2024; D'Arcy et al., 2024), including summarizing research paper content (Collins et al., 2017), detecting inaccuracies (Nuijten et al., 2016), and identifying fairness disparities (Zhang et al., 2022). Hosseini & Horbach (2023) conducted small-scale qualitative experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of ChatGPT in the peer review process, while Robertson (2023) invited 10 participants to assess the benefits of GPT-4 in assisting with peer review. Lu et al. (2024) and Tyser et al. (2024) used GPT-4 to evaluate full-text PDFs of scientific papers. However, when LLMs act as judges, even the most advanced models, such as GPT-4 (Achiam et al., 2023) and Gemini (Reid et al., 2024), still lag behind reward models specifically trained for the task, as seen in RewardBench (Lambert et al., 2024). This gap highlights the challenge of achieving human-level judgment and reasoning in AI-driven peer reviews. In contrast, we train a Generative Reward Model (Zhang et al., 2024) to simulate a comprehensive peer review. Our CycleReviewer simulates reviewers with varying perspectives, documenting summaries, strengths, and weaknesses. In the final stage, a primary reviewer consolidates these insights to deliver the final decision.

s2_metadata.title: AI for Open Science: A Multi-Agent Perspective for Ethically Translating Data to Knowledge

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper introduces AI for Open Science (AI4OS) as a multi-agent extension of AI4Science, formalizing openness through a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD)-based language that models collaborative knowledge translation across disjoint scientific agents. It proposes a MaDiSS framework with three key stages—prior knowledge communication, pattern sharing between agents, and collective knowledge review—and introduces a theoretical openness metric grounded in KDD stages and ethical principles to ensure equitable, transparent, and socially beneficial outcomes in self-driving labs and AI-driven research systems.

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces TOMATO (Automated Open-domain Hypothetical Induction), a novel NLP task where LLMs generate novel, valid scientific hypotheses from raw web corpora without manual selection or commonsense priors. Proposes the first dataset for social science hypothesis discovery using 50 recent journal papers, with raw web passages as input. Develops MOOSE, a multi-module LLM framework with three feedback mechanisms—present, past, and future feedback—that iteratively refine hypothesis generation, outperforming baseline LLMs in both GPT-4 and expert evaluations by enabling self-critique, retrospective validation, and cross-module justification.

references.raw_text: Zhang, Jiayao, Zhang, Hongming, Deng, Zhun, and Roth, Dan. Investigating fairness disparities in peer review: A language model enhanced approach. arXiv preprint arXiv:2211.06398, 2022.
references.title: Investigating Fairness Disparities in Peer Review: A Language Model Enhanced Approach

s2_metadata.title: Investigating Fairness Disparities in Peer Review: A Language Model Enhanced Approach

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper presents a comprehensive dataset of ICLR peer reviews (2017–2022) aggregated from OpenReview, arXiv, and CSRanking, enriched with high-level textual features extracted via large language models (LLMs). It evaluates fairness disparities across author gender, geography, and institutional prestige using demographic parity, equalized odds, and AUC difference metrics, finding that textual features significantly improve predictive accuracy and reduce bias compared to baseline models. The study reveals that while disparities exist (e.g., North American dominance), they are not statistically compelling; however, the review generation and scoring tasks remain challenging for current LMs, highlighting the need for new NLP methods in automated peer review.

references.raw_text: Zhang, Lunjun, Hosseini, Arian, Bansal, Hritik, Kazemi, Mehran, Kumar, Aviral, and Agarwal, Rishabh. Generative verifiers: Reward modeling as next-token prediction. arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.15240, 2024.
references.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

s2_metadata.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes Generative Reward Models (GenRM) trained via next-token prediction instead of discriminative scoring, enabling LLMs to perform reasoning-based verification through chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales. GenRM-CoT explicitly generates verification rationales before predicting correctness, allowing for majority voting at test time to improve accuracy—outperforming discriminative RMs, LLM-as-a-Judge, and self-consistency by 20–30 percentage points on GSM8K and MATH/MMLU, with significant gains in error detection for subtle logical flaws (e.g., misreading 'each' in pricing problems). The method leverages inherent generative capabilities of LLMs for unified instruction tuning, CoT reasoning, and scalable verification via test-time inference.

topics.label: LLMs for Automated Scientific Research
topics.summary: Surveys and systems that use LLMs to automate scientific research processes including hypothesis generation, experiment planning, writing, and peer review; highlights need for open-source trainable models.

claims.text: Luo et al. [1] present the first systematic survey of LLM applications across the full research cycle, including hypothesis discovery, experiment planning, scientific writing, and peer review.

The AI Scientist [2] introduces a fully automated pipeline for generating research ideas, writing and executing code, producing experimental results, composing papers, and performing simulated peer review.
MLR-Copilot [3] proposes a three-phase LLM-agent framework combining IdeaAgent for hypothesis generation and ExperimentAgent for implementation with iterative debugging.
Most existing LLM research systems rely on commercial closed-source models, which limits reproducibility and prevents reinforcement learning-based policy optimization.
Si et al. [4] conduct the first large-scale human study comparing LLM-generated and human-expert research ideas.
LLM-generated research ideas are rated as more novel but slightly less feasible than human-expert ideas.
LLMs exhibit critical limitations in self-evaluation and generation diversity for research ideas.
There is a need for trainable, open-source models that can be refined through iterative feedback.
Liu and Shah [5] find that LLMs show promise for error detection and checklist verification but struggle with holistic quality judgments in peer review.
MARG [6] is a multi-agent review generation framework that enables full-text processing beyond context-length limits and produces specific, helpful feedback.
SEA [7] is a standardized framework for automated peer review that normalizes heterogeneous reviews and incorporates self-correction via mismatch scoring.
Tyser et al. [8] develop AI-driven review systems with bias-aware design, meta-prompting, and role-playing strategies.
Zeng et al. [9] apply reinforcement learning to train review models with composite reward functions for improved factual grounding and analytical depth.
Surveys on LLM-as-a-Judge [10, 11] highlight challenges in consistency, bias, and domain generalization.
Current peer review approaches typically treat review as a standalone task, not integrated into a closed-loop system for iterative improvement.
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [12] reparameterizes the reward function to enable direct policy learning from preference pairs via a classification loss.
SimPO [13] introduces a reference-free reward based on the average log probability of sequences, improving alignment performance.
Self-Rewarding Language Models [14] propose an iterative framework where the LLM generates its own preference data for DPO training, improving instruction-following and self-evaluation.

Dong et al. [15] present a practical workflow for online iterative RLHF using proxy preference models, demonstrating that iterative policy updates with freshly sampled data outperform offline approaches.

The authors' work extends preference optimization to scientific research by constructing a self-contained iterative loop where a reviewer model provides preference signals to optimize a researcher model.
AutoSurvey [16] automates comprehensive literature survey generation through a multi-LLM pipeline with retrieval-augmented generation.
Luo et al. [1] systematically review LLM applications in scientific writing, including citation generation, abstract composition, and full-paper drafting.

The authors' work differs by training models to generate complete research papers with structured outlines, simulated experimental results, and LaTeX formatting, conditioned on references and abstracts.
Fast-DetectGPT [17] is an efficient zero-shot detector based on conditional probability curvature that distinguishes human and machine-authored text without training data or watermarking.
Fast-DetectGPT achieves substantial improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency over prior perturbation-based methods such as DetectGPT [18].
The authors adopt Fast-DetectGPT as a component of ethical safeguards to ensure transparency regarding AI-generated research outputs.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] DetectGPT leverages the negative curvature property of large language models' log probability functions—where LLM-generated text tends to reside in regions of negative curvature (e.g., local maxima)—to detect machine-generated text without training, datasets, or watermarking. It computes the average log probability of a passage under the source model versus perturbed versions generated by a generic model (e.g., T5), using the log probability ratio to identify if the original text lies in a high-curvature region typical of LLM outputs. This curvature-based approach outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving 0.95 AUROC in detecting GPT-NeoX-generated fake news versus 0.81 for prior methods.

The increasing fluency and widespread usage of large language models (LLMs) highlight the desirability of corresponding tools aiding detection of LLM-generated text. In this paper, we identify a property of the structure of an LLM's probability function that is useful for such detection. Specifically, we demonstrate that text sampled from an LLM tends to occupy negative curvature regions of the model's log probability function. Leveraging this observation, we then define a new curvature-based criterion for judging if a passage is generated from a given LLM. This approach, which we call DetectGPT, does not require training a separate classifier, collecting a dataset of real or generated passages, or explicitly watermarking generated text. It uses only log probabilities computed by the model of interest and random perturbations of the passage from another generic pre-trained language model (e.g., T5). We find DetectGPT is more discriminative than existing zero-shot methods for model sample detection, notably improving detection of fake news articles generated by 20B parameter GPT-NeoX from 0.81 AUROC for the strongest zero-shot baseline to 0.95 AUROC for DetectGPT. See https://ericmitchell.ai/detectgpt for code, data, and other project information.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] Fast-DetectGPT introduces a novel zero-shot detection framework based on conditional probability curvature, which captures the distinct token selection patterns between humans and machines. Instead of perturbing text via expensive model calls (as in DetectGPT), it efficiently samples alternative word choices and evaluates their conditional probabilities, reducing computational cost by 340x while improving detection accuracy by ~75% in both white-box and black-box settings, with superior performance in black-box scenarios where it outperforms DetectGPT's white-box results by 28%.

Large language models (LLMs) have shown the ability to produce fluent and cogent content, presenting both productivity opportunities and societal risks. To build trustworthy AI systems, it is imperative to distinguish between machine-generated and human-authored content. The leading zero-shot detector, DetectGPT, showcases commendable performance but is marred by its intensive computational costs. In this paper, we introduce the concept of conditional probability curvature to elucidate discrepancies in word choices between LLMs and humans within a given context. Utilizing this curvature as a foundational metric, we present **Fast-DetectGPT**, an optimized zero-shot detector, which substitutes DetectGPT's perturbation step with a more efficient sampling step. Our evaluations on various datasets, source models, and test conditions indicate that Fast-DetectGPT not only surpasses DetectGPT by a relative around 75% in both the white-box and black-box settings but also accelerates the detection process by a factor of 340, as detailed in Table 1. See \\url{https://github.com/baoguangsheng/fast-detect-gpt} for code, data, and results.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] DetectGPT leverages the negative curvature property of large language models' log probability functions—where LLM-generated text tends to reside in regions of negative curvature (e.g., local maxima)—to detect machine-generated text without training, datasets, or watermarking. It computes the average log probability of a passage under the source model versus perturbed versions generated by a generic model (e.g., T5), using the log probability ratio to identify if the original text lies in a high-curvature region typical of LLM outputs. This curvature-based approach outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving 0.95 AUROC in detecting GPT-NeoX-generated fake news versus 0.81 for prior methods.

The increasing fluency and widespread usage of large language models (LLMs) highlight the desirability of corresponding tools aiding detection of LLM-generated text. In this paper, we identify a property of the structure of an LLM's probability function that is useful for such detection. Specifically, we demonstrate that text sampled from an LLM tends to occupy negative curvature regions of the model's log probability function. Leveraging this observation, we then define a new curvature-based criterion for judging if a passage is generated from a given LLM. This approach, which we call DetectGPT, does not require training a separate classifier, collecting a dataset of real or generated passages, or explicitly watermarking generated text. It uses only log probabilities computed by the model of interest and random perturbations of the passage from another generic pre-trained language model (e.g., T5). We find DetectGPT is more discriminative than existing zero-shot methods for model sample detection, notably improving detection of fake news articles generated by 20B parameter GPT-NeoX from 0.81 AUROC for the strongest zero-shot baseline to 0.95 AUROC for DetectGPT. See https://ericmitchell.ai/detectgpt for code, data, and other project information.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] Fast-DetectGPT introduces a novel zero-shot detection framework based on conditional probability curvature, which captures the distinct token selection patterns between humans and machines. Instead of perturbing text via expensive model calls (as in DetectGPT), it efficiently samples alternative word choices and evaluates their conditional probabilities, reducing computational cost by 340x while improving detection accuracy by ~75% in both white-box and black-box settings, with superior performance in black-box scenarios where it outperforms DetectGPT's white-box results by 28%.

Large language models (LLMs) have shown the ability to produce fluent and cogent content, presenting both productivity opportunities and societal risks. To build trustworthy AI systems, it is imperative to distinguish between machine-generated and human-authored content. The leading zero-shot detector, DetectGPT, showcases commendable performance but is marred by its intensive computational costs. In this paper, we introduce the concept of conditional probability curvature to elucidate discrepancies in word choices between LLMs and humans within a given context. Utilizing this curvature as a foundational metric, we present **Fast-DetectGPT**, an optimized zero-shot detector, which substitutes DetectGPT's perturbation step with a more efficient sampling step. Our evaluations on various datasets, source models, and test conditions indicate that Fast-DetectGPT not only surpasses DetectGPT by a relative around 75% in both the white-box and black-box settings but also accelerates the detection process by a factor of 340, as detailed in Table 1. See \\url{https://github.com/baoguangsheng/fast-detect-gpt} for code, data, and results.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] DetectGPT leverages the negative curvature property of large language models' log probability functions—where LLM-generated text tends to reside in regions of negative curvature (e.g., local maxima)—to detect machine-generated text without training, datasets, or watermarking. It computes the average log probability of a passage under the source model versus perturbed versions generated by a generic model (e.g., T5), using the log probability ratio to identify if the original text lies in a high-curvature region typical of LLM outputs. This curvature-based approach outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving 0.95 AUROC in detecting GPT-NeoX-generated fake news versus 0.81 for prior methods.

The increasing fluency and widespread usage of large language models (LLMs) highlight the desirability of corresponding tools aiding detection of LLM-generated text. In this paper, we identify a property of the structure of an LLM's probability function that is useful for such detection. Specifically, we demonstrate that text sampled from an LLM tends to occupy negative curvature regions of the model's log probability function. Leveraging this observation, we then define a new curvature-based criterion for judging if a passage is generated from a given LLM. This approach, which we call DetectGPT, does not require training a separate classifier, collecting a dataset of real or generated passages, or explicitly watermarking generated text. It uses only log probabilities computed by the model of interest and random perturbations of the passage from another generic pre-trained language model (e.g., T5). We find DetectGPT is more discriminative than existing zero-shot methods for model sample detection, notably improving detection of fake news articles generated by 20B parameter GPT-NeoX from 0.81 AUROC for the strongest zero-shot baseline to 0.95 AUROC for DetectGPT. See https://ericmitchell.ai/detectgpt for code, data, and other project information.

retrieved_evidence.text: [research paper] Fast-DetectGPT introduces a novel zero-shot detection framework based on conditional probability curvature, which captures the distinct token selection patterns between humans and machines. Instead of perturbing text via expensive model calls (as in DetectGPT), it efficiently samples alternative word choices and evaluates their conditional probabilities, reducing computational cost by 340x while improving detection accuracy by ~75% in both white-box and black-box settings, with superior performance in black-box scenarios where it outperforms DetectGPT's white-box results by 28%.

Large language models (LLMs) have shown the ability to produce fluent and cogent content, presenting both productivity opportunities and societal risks. To build trustworthy AI systems, it is imperative to distinguish between machine-generated and human-authored content. The leading zero-shot detector, DetectGPT, showcases commendable performance but is marred by its intensive computational costs. In this paper, we introduce the concept of conditional probability curvature to elucidate discrepancies in word choices between LLMs and humans within a given context. Utilizing this curvature as a foundational metric, we present **Fast-DetectGPT**, an optimized zero-shot detector, which substitutes DetectGPT's perturbation step with a more efficient sampling step. Our evaluations on various datasets, source models, and test conditions indicate that Fast-DetectGPT not only surpasses DetectGPT by a relative around 75% in both the white-box and black-box settings but also accelerates the detection process by a factor of 340, as detailed in Table 1. See \\url{https://github.com/baoguangsheng/fast-detect-gpt} for code, data, and results.
